subject: Physics
solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through potential V is 

λ=hmv=h2 m(KE)=h2 meV

where, h is Planck's constant, e is charge of electron and m is mass of electron.

From above relation, λ∝1V

∴ λλ0=2040

⇒ λ=λ02.

subject: Physics
solution:
$\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi} \\
& \mathrm{mv}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}} \\
& \lambda=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{rh}}{\mathrm{nh}} \\
& \lambda \propto \frac{\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{n}} \\
& \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_1 \mathrm{n}_4}{\mathrm{n}_1 \mathrm{r}_4}=\frac{5.3 \times 10^{-11} \times 4}{1 \times 84.8 \times 10^{-11}} \\
& \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_4}=\frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
mV=m V A + m 2 V B

V= V A + V B 2    −−−−−−−−−−−−  (i)

V= V B − V A     −−−−−−−−−−−−  (ii)

Solving (i) and (ii)

2V= 3 V B 2

∴ V B = 4V 3

V A = V B −V= V 3

P A = mV 3

P B = m 2 . 4V 3

λ A λ B = h× P B P A ×h = 2mV 3 mV 3 =2

subject: Physics
solution:
The root mean squared velocity of a gas is given by 

v=3RTM

⇒v∝T

Let

 T1=300 KT2=600 K

Taking velocity ratios at the given temperatures,

v1v2=T1T2=300600=12

The de Broglie wavelength is given by λ=hmv. So,

λ∝1v

The ratio of the wavelengths is 

λ1λ2=v2v1=21

⇒λ2=12λ1

subject: Physics
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$K_1 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_1} - \phi$

$K_2 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_2} - \phi$

Given $\lambda_1 = 2\lambda_2$, the first equation becomes:

$K_1 = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda_2} - \phi$

Multiplying by $2$:

$2K_1 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_2} - 2\phi$

Substituting $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_2} = K_2 + \phi$ from the second equation:

$2K_1 = (K_2 + \phi) - 2\phi$

$2K_1 = K_2 - \phi$

$\phi = K_2 - 2K_1$

Answer: $K_2-2K_1$

solution:
For α particle, mα=4 a.m.u and charge qα=2e.

For proton, mp=1 a.m.u and charge qp=e.

As the charged particle are moving through same potential, the kinetic energy acquired by the particle will be equal to electric potential energy i.e., 12mv2=qV, where, V is potential.

Using the relation between momentum p and kinetic energy, we have 

p=2mE=2mqV

Then, the ratio of linear momenta of two particles is pαpp=mαqαmpqp=41×21

=221=22:1

subject: Physics
options: All the metals will give out photo electrons with same kinetic energies.
solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons is given by:

$K_{ ext{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of incident radiation and $\Phi$ is the work function of the metal.

The work function is related to the threshold frequency $ u_0$ by $\Phi = h u_0$. From the given graph, the x-intercept represents the threshold frequency. We can observe that:

$ u_0(X_1) < u_0(X_2) < u_0(X_3)$

Therefore, the work functions of the metals follow the order:

$\Phi(X_1) < \Phi(X_2) < \Phi(X_3)$

For the same wavelength $\lambda$ of incident radiation, the term $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ is constant. Thus, the kinetic energy $K_{ ext{max}}$ will be greatest for the metal with the smallest work function.

Since metal $X_1$ has the minimum work function, it will emit electrons with the greatest kinetic energy.

Answer: $X_1$

subject: Physics
solution:
Light contains two frequencies: $\omega_1 = 3 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s and $\omega_2 = 12 \times 10^{15}$ rad/s
For maximum KE, use higher frequency $\omega_2$.
Energy: $E_2 = \frac{h\omega_2}{2\pi} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 12 \times 10^{15}}{2\pi}$
$= \frac{79.2 \times 10^{-19}}{6.28} = 12.6 \times 10^{-19}$ J $= 7.9$ eV
$KE_{max} = E_2 - \phi = 7.9 - 2.8 = 5.1$ eV

subject: Physics
solution:
Energy in joule (E)
$=$ charge $\times$ potential diff. in volt $\mathrm{E}_{\text {electron }}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{V}$ and $\mathrm{E}_{\text {proton }}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}} 4 \mathrm{~V}$
de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mE}}}$
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\lambda_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{eV}}} \text { and } \lambda_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{e} 4 \mathrm{~V}}} \\
\left(\because \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{P}}\right)
\end{array}
$$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{e}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{P}}} & =\frac{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{eV}}}}{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{P}} \mathrm{e} 4 \mathrm{~V}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{P}} \mathrm{e} 4 \mathrm{~V}}{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{eV}}} \\ & =2 \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
The de Broglie wavelength of an electron in free space is given by $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{mv}$.

When the electron enters the medium, its velocity is reduced by $20\%$. The new velocity is $v' = v - 0.20v = 0.80v$.

The de Broglie wavelength in the medium is $\lambda' = \dfrac{h}{mv'}$.

Substituting $v' = 0.80v$, we get $\lambda' = \dfrac{h}{m(0.80v)} = \dfrac{1}{0.80} \left(\dfrac{h}{mv}\right)$.

Since \dfrac{1}{0.80} = \dfrac{100}{80} = 1.25$, we have $\lambda' = 1.25\lambda_0$.

Comparing this with $\lambda' = \alpha\lambda_0$, we get $\alpha = 1.25$.

Answer: $1.25$

Since f02<f0 i.e. the incident frequency is less than threshold frequency. Hence, there will be no emission of photoelectrons.

Therefore, current =0 and no photo electrons will be emitted.

subject: Physics
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$eV_s = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

For the first case:

$3eV_0 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

For the second case:

$eV_0 = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda} - \Phi$

Multiplying the second equation by $3$ and equating with the first equation:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi = \dfrac{3hc}{2\lambda} - 3\Phi$

$2\Phi = \dfrac{3hc}{2\lambda} - \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

$2\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda}$

$\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{4\lambda}$

Since the work function $\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_0}$, where $\lambda_0$ is the threshold wavelength:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_0} = \dfrac{hc}{4\lambda}$

$\lambda_0 = 4\lambda$

Given $\lambda_0 = \alpha\lambda$, we get $\alpha = 4$.

Answer: $4$
